text: We investigate multi-spacecraft observations of the January 17, 2010 solar energetic particle event. Energetic electrons and protons have been observed over a remarkable large longitudinal range at the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO suggesting a longitudinal spread of nearly 360 degrees at 1AU. The flaring active region, which was on the backside of the Sun as seen from Earth, was separated by more than 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of each of the three spacecraft. The event is characterized by strongly delayed energetic particle onsets with respect to the flare and only small or no anisotropies in the intensity measurements at all three locations. The presence of a coronal shock is evidenced by the observation of a type II radio burst from the Earth and STEREO B. In order to describe the observations in terms of particle transport in the interplanetary medium, including perpendicular diffusion, a 1D model describing the propagation along a magnetic field line (model 1) (Dr\"oge, 2003) and the 3D propagation model (model 2) by (Dr\"oge et al., 2010) including perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium have been applied, respectively. While both models are capable of reproducing the observations, model 1 requires injection functions at the Sun of several hours. Model 2, which includes lateral transport in the solar wind, reveals high values for the ratio of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. Because we do not find evidence for unusual long injection functions at the Sun we favor a scenario with strong perpendicular transport in the interplanetary medium as explanation for the observations.

This study examines multi-spacecraft observations of the solar energetic particle event on January 17, 2010. Observations of energetic electrons and protons were recorded across an extensive longitudinal range by the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO, indicating a nearly 360-degree longitudinal distribution at 1 AU. The active region responsible for the flare was located on the far side of the Sun relative to Earth and was separated by over 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of all three spacecraft. The event exhibited significantly delayed onsets of energetic particles relative to the flare, along with minimal or no anisotropies in intensity measurements at all three spacecraft locations. 

Evidence of a coronal shock was provided by the detection of a type II radio burst by both Earth-based instruments and STEREO B. To interpret these observations within the framework of particle transport in the interplanetary medium, including the effects of perpendicular diffusion, two models were utilized. The first model (Dröge, 2003) describes particle propagation along a single magnetic field line in one dimension. The second model (Dröge et al., 2010) incorporates three-dimensional propagation and accounts for perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium. Both models were able to replicate the observations; however, model 1 necessitated injection functions at the Sun spanning several hours. In contrast, model 2, which includes lateral transport in the solar wind, indicated high ratios of perpendicular to parallel diffusion.

Since there is no evidence to support the presence of unusually prolonged injection functions at the Sun, the observations are more consistently explained by a scenario involving strong perpendicular transport of particles in the interplanetary medium.

This study dives into the solar tempest unleashed on January 17, 2010, tracing its fiery echoes through the cosmos with a fleet of spacecraft—STEREO A, STEREO B, and SOHO—each stationed like watchful sentinels across an immense swath of the Sun’s perimeter. The drama unfolded with a nearly complete 360-degree spread of energetic electrons and protons at 1 AU, painting the solar system with a vivid, sweeping stroke. The flare’s birthplace? A restless patch of solar fury hidden on the far side of the Sun, glaring over 100 degrees of longitude away from the magnetic footpoints tethered to the three observing spacecraft. Yet, despite the distance, its charged emissaries arrived late and weary, their onsets sluggish, their intensities eerily isotropic—no sharp blasts, no favored directions, just a quiet diffusion through space.

Clues to the source of this cosmic choreography emerged with the faint, ghostly whisper of a type II radio burst, caught not just by Earth-bound ears but also by STEREO B. This signal hinted at the presence of a coronal shock, a wavefront slicing through the Sun’s upper atmosphere like the prow of a ship through turbulent seas. To decode the puzzle of how these particles wandered so far and wide, two models stepped into the fray. One, a one-dimensional guide (Dröge, 2003), confined particles to a single magnetic highway, needing hours-long injections at the Sun to match the observations. The other, a three-dimensional navigator (Dröge et al., 2010), embraced the chaotic crosswinds of the interplanetary medium, where particles drift laterally in a dance of perpendicular diffusion.

The evidence tilted the scales: no signs of prolonged injections from the Sun’s surface emerged. Instead, the observations sang a clearer tune—a tale of particles swept sideways and scattered broadly by the solar wind, their path shaped by the strong hand of perpendicular transport.

On January 17, 2010, the Sun unleashed a tempest of charged particles, its disruptive influence unfurling across the vast tapestry of interplanetary space. A triad of spacecraft—STEREO A, STEREO B, and SOHO—stood as vigilant outposts, strategically arrayed along the Sun’s sprawling circumference, each poised to witness the cosmic repercussions of this solar outburst. What transpired was nothing short of a celestial panorama: a nearly complete encirclement of energetic electrons and protons propagating through the heliosphere at 1 AU, their dispersion an expansive brushstroke of raw energy. The origin of this eruption lay hidden on the Sun’s far side, shrouded in solitude over 100 degrees of longitude distant from the magnetic conduits connecting the spacecraft to their solar source. And yet, despite this spatial chasm, the particles arrived with muted vitality—a sluggish procession, their intensities homogenized into a diffuse isotropy, devoid of the directional vigor or concentrated bursts often emblematic of such phenomena.

The enigma of this widespread dissemination found its first breadcrumb in the spectral murmur of a type II radio burst, a signal of fleeting fragility detected not only by terrestrial instruments but also by STEREO B. This ethereal trace, like the distant echo of thunder, betrayed the presence of a coronal shock, a dynamic wavefront thrusting through the Sun’s outer layers akin to the prow of a ship slicing through turbulent seas. To unravel the mechanisms governing the particles’ vast dispersal, two theoretical frameworks were summoned to the analytical stage. The first, a one-dimensional construct (Dröge, 2003), envisioned particles strictly confined to magnetic field lines, requiring extended injections from the Sun to reconcile with the observed arrival patterns. The second, a three-dimensional paradigm (Dröge et al., 2010), embraced the stochastic interplay of the solar wind and interplanetary turbulence, wherein particles diffused laterally in a chaotic ballet of perpendicular transport.

The empirical verdict was decisive: no evidence supported the prolonged solar injections posited by the one-dimensional model. Instead, the observations illuminated a different narrative—one of particles swept and scattered by the solar wind’s omnipresent crosscurrents, their trajectories shaped by the intricate dynamics of perpendicular diffusion.

On January 17, 2010, the Sun sent out a burst of charged particles, spreading its effects across the vast stretch of space around it. Three spacecraft—STEREO A, STEREO B, and SOHO—were positioned at different points around the Sun, ready to observe what happened. Together, they captured an impressive event: a nearly complete circle of energetic electrons and protons moving through space at Earth’s distance from the Sun. These particles spread out widely, carrying energy across a large area. The eruption that caused this happened on the far side of the Sun, more than 100 degrees away from the magnetic paths connecting the spacecraft to their source. Even so, the particles eventually reached the spacecraft, though they arrived slowly and evenly spread out, without the sharp bursts or clear direction that these events sometimes show.

Scientists found their first clue about how the particles spread so widely in a type II radio burst. This faint signal, picked up by both instruments on Earth and STEREO B, pointed to a coronal shock—essentially a wave pushing through the outer layers of the Sun, like the front of a ship cutting through choppy waters. To figure out how the particles dispersed so far, researchers tested two different ideas. The first idea, based on a one-dimensional model (Dröge, 2003), suggested that the particles stayed confined to magnetic field lines and would require a long-lasting release of energy from the Sun to match the observations. The second idea, a three-dimensional model (Dröge et al., 2010), took into account the swirling turbulence of the solar wind, proposing that the particles could move sideways in unpredictable ways, spreading out as they traveled.

The data made the answer clear: there was no evidence to support the idea of a long, steady release of particles from the Sun, as the first model suggested. Instead, the observations showed something different. The particles were scattered and carried along by the chaotic movements of the solar wind, their paths shaped by the complex process of spreading sideways through space.

Positioned at different vantage points around the Sun, three spacecraft—STEREO A, STEREO B, and SOHO—were prepared to observe the phenomenon. These spacecraft collectively recorded a notable occurrence: a nearly complete distribution of energetic electrons and protons moving through space at a distance equivalent to Earth's orbit. The particles dispersed over a wide area, transporting energy across an extensive region. The eruption responsible for this event originated on the far side of the Sun, at an angle exceeding 100 degrees from the magnetic connections linking the spacecraft to their source. Despite this significant angular displacement, the particles eventually reached the spacecraft. Their arrival, however, was gradual and uniform, lacking the concentrated bursts or directional precision often associated with such events.

The first indication of how these particles achieved such broad dispersion was found in a type II radio burst. This weak signal, detected by ground-based instruments and by STEREO B, indicated the presence of a coronal shock. A coronal shock is a wave propagating through the Sun's outer layers, analogous to the leading edge of a ship moving through turbulent waters. To determine the mechanism behind the particles' extensive distribution, researchers evaluated two explanatory models. The first model, based on a one-dimensional framework (Dröge, 2003), proposed that the particles remained confined to magnetic field lines and that their widespread distribution required a prolonged release of energy from the Sun. The second model, employing a three-dimensional framework (Dröge et al., 2010), incorporated the turbulent dynamics of the solar wind, suggesting that the particles could undergo lateral displacement in unpredictable directions as they traveled.

The observational data provided a definitive conclusion. There was no support for the hypothesis of a sustained, steady release of particles, as posited by the first model. Instead, the evidence indicated that the particles were scattered and transported by the chaotic turbulence of the solar wind. Their trajectories were influenced by the complex processes of lateral diffusion as they propagated through space.

The January 17, 2010 solar energetic particle event unfolded like a cosmic symphony, its notes carried on the breath of the solar wind, reverberating across an astonishingly vast stage. Energetic electrons and protons surged outward, painting the interplanetary canvas with their presence, their spread so immense that they spanned nearly the full circle of the Sun’s embrace—almost 360 degrees at 1 AU. The two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO found themselves witnesses to this celestial performance, despite their magnetic roots lying more than 100 degrees in longitude away from the Sun’s flaring active region, which smoldered unseen on the Sun’s far side from Earth’s perspective.

Yet, this was no ordinary flare-and-particle show. The particles arrived late to the party, their onsets lagging conspicuously behind the flare, and they meandered in their intensity, showing little to no directional bias at any of the spacecraft’s locations. Overhead, a coronal shock made its presence known, trumpeted by a type II radio burst, detectable both from Earth and from STEREO B, like a distant horn announcing turbulence in the Sun’s upper atmosphere.

To decode this intricate web of motion, two models stepped forward: one (model 1) tracing the path of particles along magnetic field lines with a one-dimensional precision, and another (model 2) weaving a richer, three-dimensional tapestry of propagation that accounted for sideways wanderings—perpendicular diffusion—through the solar wind. Each model wrestled with the data, striving to make sense of it. While the first could mimic the observations, it demanded an unreasonably prolonged particle injection at the Sun, stretching for hours. The second, embracing the chaos of lateral transport, pointed instead to a high ratio of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. With no sign of such drawn-out injections at the Sun, the evidence leaned heavily on the side of strong cross-field diffusion in the interplanetary medium, a dance of particles scattering wide and free.

The solar energetic particle event of January 17, 2010, unfolded as a complex interaction of solar phenomena, with particles carried outward by the solar wind across an expansive region of space. Energetic electrons and protons propagated so extensively that their distribution nearly encompassed the entire 360-degree circumference of the Sun at 1 AU. Observations were recorded by the two STEREO spacecraft and the SOHO observatory, despite their magnetic field connections being more than 100 degrees in longitude away from the originating active region of the Sun. This region, located on the Sun’s far side relative to Earth, was obscured from direct observation.

This event deviated significantly from typical solar flare and particle dynamics. The arrival of particles was notably delayed relative to the associated solar flare, and their intensities exhibited minimal directional variation across the locations of the observing spacecraft. Concurrently, a coronal shock wave was detected, indicated by a type II radio burst observable from both Earth and STEREO B. This radio signature served as evidence of a disturbance within the Sun's upper atmospheric layers.

Two theoretical models were applied to analyze the particle propagation. The first model employed a one-dimensional approach, assuming particles traveled strictly along magnetic field lines. The second model incorporated a three-dimensional framework, which included lateral particle motion, known as perpendicular diffusion, through the solar wind. The one-dimensional model was able to replicate the observed particle distributions, but only by assuming an improbably extended period of particle injection at the Sun, lasting several hours. In contrast, the three-dimensional model, which accounted for significant cross-field diffusion, did not require such prolonged injection durations. Given the absence of evidence supporting extended particle injection at the Sun, the findings strongly supported the second model. This conclusion suggested that the observed particle distribution was primarily shaped by enhanced perpendicular diffusion within the interplanetary medium, resulting in a broad and isotropic spread of particles.

On January 17, 2010, a solar energetic particle event unfolded in a fascinating way. Particles were carried outward by the solar wind, spreading across a huge area of space. Energetic electrons and protons traveled so widely that they covered nearly the entire 360-degree circle around the Sun at Earth's distance. Scientists tracked this event using the two STEREO spacecraft and the SOHO observatory, even though these instruments were positioned over 100 degrees in longitude away from the active solar region that started it all. This region was on the far side of the Sun, hidden from direct view from Earth.

This event didn’t follow the usual patterns of solar flares or particle behavior. For one, the particles took longer than normal to arrive after the related solar flare. Also, their intensity was nearly the same across all the spacecraft locations, showing little variation. At the same time, scientists detected a coronal shock wave, which was confirmed by a type II radio burst seen both from Earth and STEREO B. This radio signal was evidence of a disturbance high up in the Sun’s atmosphere.

To understand how the particles moved, researchers tested two different models. The first model assumed particles traveled strictly along the Sun’s magnetic field lines, in a straight, one-dimensional path. The second model allowed for a more complex three-dimensional movement, including sideways motion through the solar wind, called perpendicular diffusion. The first model could explain the spread of particles, but only if the Sun had been releasing them for an unusually long time—several hours. The second model, which included the sideways motion, didn’t require such a long release period. Since there was no evidence showing the Sun was injecting particles for hours, the second model made more sense. It showed that the wide and even spread of particles was mainly due to strong perpendicular diffusion in space, which caused the particles to spread out in all directions.

On January 17, 2010, a solar energetic particle event unfolded with a remarkable departure from conventional expectations. Propelled outward by the solar wind, charged electrons and protons dispersed across an immense swath of space, achieving a near-complete 360-degree encirclement of the Sun at Earth's orbital radius. Observational efforts by the STEREO spacecraft duo and the SOHO observatory proved pivotal, despite these instruments being stationed over 100 degrees in solar longitude from the locus of activity—a dynamic region concealed on the Sun’s far side, imperceptible from Earth’s vantage point.

This event defied established paradigms governing solar flare-induced particle behavior. Notably, a significant temporal delay separated the associated solar flare from the particles' arrival, a divergence from typical rapid-response timelines. Furthermore, the energetic particle intensities displayed an unusual uniformity across all observational points, with minimal spatial variation. Concurrently, researchers identified the presence of a coronal shock wave, corroborated by the detection of a type II radio burst originating from elevated layers of the solar atmosphere. This burst was captured both by Earth-based instruments and the STEREO B spacecraft, marking a propagative disturbance within the Sun’s upper regions.

To unravel the mechanics behind this unprecedented dispersal, scientists interrogated two distinct theoretical frameworks. The first posited a linear, magnetic-field-constrained trajectory for the particles, wherein their motion adhered strictly to one-dimensional flux tubes emanating from the Sun. The second model, by contrast, embraced a more intricate three-dimensional perspective, incorporating lateral motion through the turbulent medium of the solar wind—an effect termed perpendicular diffusion. While the linear model could account for the expansive spread of particles, it necessitated an implausibly prolonged release period from the Sun, spanning several hours. In contrast, the diffusion-oriented model sidestepped such temporal improbabilities, attributing the observed uniformity and breadth of particle distribution to robust lateral scattering processes within interplanetary space.

Ultimately, the findings converged on the second model as the more parsimonious explanation, underscoring the decisive role of perpendicular diffusion in facilitating the particles' omnidirectional dispersion—a phenomenon that reshaped the conventional understanding of solar particle dynamics.

The Sun, on that January day in 2010, became an unbound maestro, orchestrating a cosmic symphony that bent the rules of its own playbook. A torrent of solar particles—electrons and protons charged like tiny electrified messengers—was flung into the void, spreading not in tidy arcs but in a wild, all-encompassing sweep, wrapping themselves around the Sun like an invisible halo stretched to the orbital edge of Earth. Even from their distant perches, over 100 degrees away in solar longitude, the watchful instruments aboard STEREO and SOHO caught the echoes of this peculiar ballet, their gaze fixed on a realm hidden from Earth’s sight: the Sun’s far side, veiled and enigmatic.

This was no ordinary solar flare spectacle. The particles didn’t rush in as expected, their arrival lagging far behind the flare’s eruption—a tardy entrance that defied the usual script. And when they did appear, their intensity carried a bizarre sameness, smooth as a freshly poured surface, unbroken by the jagged variations typical in such events. Meanwhile, high above the Sun’s fiery skin, a coronal shock wave rippled outward, its presence betrayed by the whispers of a type II radio burst—a signal born in the Sun’s upper atmosphere and heard by Earth-bound detectors and the STEREO B spacecraft alike. The disturbance spread, a tremor in the solar ether.

To crack the riddle of this sprawling dispersion, scientists turned their minds to two competing theories. One, orderly and constrained, imagined the particles shackled to magnetic field lines, marching one-dimensionally along flux tubes like soldiers on a narrow bridge. But this tale required an implausible stretch of time—hours upon hours—for the Sun’s release. The other theory, unruly yet elegant, saw chaos as the key: a three-dimensional dance where particles slipped sideways, scattered by the turbulence of the solar wind in a phenomenon called perpendicular diffusion. Here, the spreading made sense, the symmetry explained by lateral jostling in interplanetary space.

In the end, this second vision carried the day, its story simple yet profound: the Sun’s particles, untethered and diffused, broke free of old conventions, revealing a new truth about their boundless, omnidirectional flight.

Water drop impacting a superhydrophobic surface (SHS) rebounds completely with remarkable elasticity. For such an impact, the balance between the inertial and capillary forces ascertain the contact time. This is found to be fairly constant for any macroscopically flat SHS and for a given drop volume. Recently, various studies have shown that breaking the radial symmetry during the drop impact can significantly reduce the contact time below that of a flat SHS. One such study has been performed on a cylindrical SHS with a curvature comparable to the drop. The reduction in contact time has been attributed to the radially anisotropic flow imparted by the tangential component of momentum and the elliptical footprint of the drop during the crash. Here, we perform drop impact experiments on bathtub-like SHS and show that the radial anisotropy can be triggered even in the absence of both the criteria mentioned above. This is shown to be a consequence of the lamella deflection during the spreading of the drop. The reduction in contact time is quite clearly evident in this experimental regime.

When a water drop impacts a superhydrophobic surface (SHS), it rebounds completely with notable elasticity. In such cases, the duration of contact is determined by the interplay between inertial and capillary forces. This contact time remains relatively constant for any macroscopically flat SHS, provided the drop volume is fixed. Recent research has demonstrated that disrupting radial symmetry during the drop impact can substantially reduce the contact time compared to that observed on a flat SHS. For example, one study investigated a cylindrical SHS with curvature comparable to the drop and attributed the reduction in contact time to radially anisotropic flow caused by the tangential momentum component and the elliptical shape of the drop’s footprint during impact. 

In this work, we conduct drop impact experiments on bathtub-like SHS and demonstrate that radial anisotropy can be induced even without the aforementioned conditions. This effect is shown to arise from the deflection of the lamella during the spreading phase of the drop. Within this experimental context, the reduction in contact time is clearly observed.

When a water droplet collides with a superhydrophobic surface—a surface so defiant of wetness it feels like magic—it springs back entirely, like a rubber ball flung against a trampoline. The encounter, fleeting and elastic, is governed by a delicate tug-of-war between inertia’s forward push and the whispering tension of capillary forces. On any smooth, flat SHS, the contact time remains largely unshaken, a steadfast constant so long as the droplet’s volume stays fixed. But here comes the twist: recent experiments have shown that if you disrupt the drop’s perfect radial symmetry, the contact time can shrink dramatically—dancing far below what’s seen on pristine flatness. One study, for instance, explored a cylindrical SHS whose curvature flirted with that of the droplet itself, finding that the shortened contact was driven by radially anisotropic flow, sculpted by tangential momentum and the elliptical footprint of impact.

In the present study, however, we take the plunge into bathtub-like SHS, discovering that radial asymmetry needs no such curvature or footprint tricks to emerge. The lamella, that thin sheet spreading from the drop, bends and deflects during its fleeting stretch, birthing anisotropy without ceremony. The result? A clear and measurable cut in contact time—a revelation, plain as water and just as quick.

When a droplet of water collides with a superhydrophobic surface—a material so impervious to wetting that it borders on the alchemical—it rebounds entirely, mimicking the elastic trajectory of a ball ricocheting off a trampoline. This ephemeral interaction is orchestrated by a finely balanced contest between the linear insistence of inertia and the subtle, cohesive pull of capillary forces. On any smooth and planar superhydrophobic surface (SHS), the temporal span of contact between droplet and substrate remains remarkably invariant, provided the volume of the droplet is held constant. Yet, a provocative deviation emerges: experimental evidence reveals that disrupting the droplet’s radial symmetry can truncate contact time significantly, reducing it to values unattainable on unblemished flatness. For instance, investigations into a cylindrical SHS—its curvature echoing that of the droplet itself—demonstrated that this contraction in contact time arises from radially anisotropic fluid dynamics, sculpted by tangential momentum and the oblong geometry of the impact zone.

In contrast, the present study ventures into the uncharted domain of bathtub-like SHS, uncovering that radial asymmetry can arise without invoking curvature or elliptical impact footprints. The lamella—the thin, rapidly spreading liquid sheet—undergoes spontaneous deflection and bending during its transient expansion, generating anisotropy without the need for external geometrical provocations. The consequence? A demonstrable reduction in contact time, as clear-cut and fleeting as the droplet’s own ephemeral existence.

When a drop of water hits a superhydrophobic surface—a material so water-repellent it seems almost magical—it bounces back completely, much like a ball springing off a trampoline. This brief interaction happens because of a delicate balance between the forward push of the droplet’s motion and the gentle pull of the water’s surface tension. On any smooth, flat superhydrophobic surface (SHS), the time the droplet spends touching the surface stays surprisingly consistent, as long as the droplet’s size doesn’t change. But here’s something interesting: if the droplet’s shape isn’t perfectly round, the contact time can be much shorter—shorter than what’s possible on a perfectly flat surface. For example, studies with a cylindrical SHS—shaped a bit like the droplet itself—showed that this shortened contact time happens because the droplet spreads out unevenly, influenced by the surface’s shape and the way the droplet hits it.

This study takes things further, looking at SHS surfaces shaped like shallow bathtubs. It found that you don’t need curves or oval-shaped impacts to create asymmetry. Instead, the thin sheet of water spreading out from the droplet bends and shifts on its own as it expands, creating an uneven spread naturally. The result? The droplet spends even less time on the surface before bouncing away, a quick and fleeting process, just like the droplet’s brief existence in motion.

Findings indicate that asymmetry in the droplet’s spread does not require curved surfaces or non-circular impact conditions. Instead, as the thin film of water expands outward from the droplet, it naturally undergoes uneven deformation due to internal dynamics. This inherent asymmetry accelerates the rebound process, leading to a shorter contact duration. Consequently, the interaction between the droplet and the surface becomes even briefer, emphasizing the transient nature of this physical phenomenon.

When a droplet of water makes contact with a superhydrophobic surface (SHS), it does not simply rest on the surface; rather, it rebounds and departs in a manner consistent with nearly perfect elastic behavior. The duration of the droplet’s interaction with the surface is governed by the interplay between inertial forces and capillary tension. For a flat SHS and a droplet of constant size, this contact time remains highly consistent. However, recent findings indicate that disrupting the radial symmetry of the droplet’s impact can significantly reduce this contact duration.

One set of findings emerged from experiments involving cylindrical SHS surfaces, whose curvature approximately aligns with the shape of the droplet. In these cases, the shortened contact time was attributed to asymmetric radial flow, which arises from the droplet’s tangential momentum and the elliptical splash pattern it leaves behind. More recently, experiments have extended these observations to SHS surfaces shaped like bathtubs. Unlike cylindrical surfaces, these do not rely on curvature or elongated footprints to induce asymmetry. Instead, the reduction in contact time stems from the behavior of the lamella—a thin liquid sheet that spreads outward upon impact. This lamella deforms and deviates, disrupting radial symmetry through a distinct mechanism. The outcome is a pronounced reduction in contact time, further substantiated by experimental evidence.

When a drop of water touches a superhydrophobic surface (SHS), it doesn’t just sit there. Instead, it bounces off quickly, acting almost like a perfectly elastic object. The time the droplet stays in contact with the surface depends on the balance between its motion and the tension in the water. On flat SHS surfaces, and with droplets of the same size, this contact time stays very steady. But recent studies show that changing the way the droplet hits the surface can make it spend even less time there.

One set of experiments looked at SHS surfaces shaped like cylinders. These surfaces curve in a way that matches the shape of the droplet, and this curvature causes the water to move unevenly outward. The droplet’s sideways motion and the way it splashes in an oval pattern help shorten the contact time. More recent tests used SHS surfaces shaped like bathtubs. These surfaces don’t rely on being curved or having stretched-out shapes to affect the droplet’s behavior. Instead, they change how the lamella—a thin sheet of liquid spreading out from the impact—moves. The lamella bends and shifts, breaking the usual even flow of the water. This new way of disrupting the droplet’s motion leads to an even shorter contact time, as confirmed by experiments.

When a droplet of water encounters a superhydrophobic surface (SHS), its behavior transcends mere passivity; it rebounds with a dynamism akin to a nearly ideal elastic collision. The temporal interval during which the droplet remains in contact with the surface emerges as a function of the interplay between its kinetic energy and the cohesive forces intrinsic to the liquid. On planar SHS substrates, this contact duration exhibits remarkable uniformity when droplet size is held constant. However, empirical investigations have recently illuminated how modifications to the droplet’s manner of impact can precipitate an even more abbreviated interaction.

In one experimental paradigm, researchers examined SHS surfaces configured as cylindrical geometries. The curvature of these surfaces approximates the droplet’s own contour, inducing an asymmetrical radial dispersion of liquid. This anisotropic motion, coupled with the elliptical splashing trajectory, serves to truncate the contact duration. More advanced explorations have since shifted focus to SHS surfaces resembling the structural topology of bathtubs. Here, the mechanism diverges fundamentally: these surfaces eschew reliance on curvature or elongation to influence droplet dynamics. Instead, they intervene in the behavior of the lamella—a tenuous liquid sheet that propagates outward upon impact. By bending and distorting the lamella, these surfaces disrupt the otherwise orderly hydrodynamic flow, thereby hastening the droplet’s departure. Experimental validation underscores the efficacy of this novel mode of interaction in achieving unprecedented reductions in contact time.

When a water droplet greets a superhydrophobic surface, it doesn’t simply land—it ricochets, springing back like a perfect gymnast executing a near-flawless vault. Its fleeting touch with the surface is a delicate dance, choreographed by the push-and-pull between the droplet’s kinetic energy and the liquid’s own adhesive grip. On flat SHS terrains, this momentary exchange is strikingly consistent, as if the droplets have agreed on a secret contract of uniformity—provided their size remains constant. But recent experiments reveal that shaking up the droplet’s landing style can slash this contact time even further.

One such study turned its gaze to SHS surfaces shaped into cylindrical forms. These curved landscapes mimic the droplet’s natural roundness, coaxing the liquid into an uneven spread—radial ripples stretching asymmetrically like a lopsided bloom. The resulting splash, elongated and elliptical, speeds up the droplet’s retreat, cutting the interaction short. Yet the innovators didn’t stop there. They ventured into stranger territory, crafting SHS surfaces that channel the geometry of bathtubs. Here, the game changes entirely: no curves, no elongation, no mimicry. Instead, these surfaces take aim at the droplet’s lamella—the fragile liquid sheet that fans outward on impact. By warping and twisting this sheet, they unravel the smooth flow of hydrodynamic order, forcing the droplet to abandon the surface in record time. Trials confirm that this bold disruption delivers contact durations shorter than anything seen before—an audacious feat of liquid manipulation.

The enduring reputation of our most esteemed comic dramatist rests almost entirely on three of his five plays. His initial comedy amounted to little more than an impressive exercise in imitation, modeled on earlier works that his own surpassed even at this nascent stage. His attempts in tragedy, however, appear artificial and unconvincing, resembling the overly adorned and exaggerated features of Lady Wishfort. Nonetheless, his three principal comedies alone suffice to guarantee a legacy as enduring as the English language itself. Without these, we would lack exemplars of comedy at its most refined and elevated.

Ben Jonson, who uniquely sought to reform the dramatic conventions of his era—when comedy and tragedy were as interwoven on the stage as they were in life—fell short of achieving the necessary fluidity and elegance in the comedic elements of his otherwise grand and intricate compositions. Among Congreve’s immediate predecessors, those who aspired to construct a distinctively English comedy on French foundations, Wycherley’s work proved too coarse in quality, while Etherege’s was too insubstantial to rival his. Beyond these, the field offered little of value, with the dominant alternative being the unpolished and forceful output of Shadwell, whose talent, though vigorous, lacked refinement.

To adapt Walter Savage Landor’s metaphor, one could assert that a fragment of Molière’s genius would have sufficed to create a Congreve, while a fragment of Congreve’s talent would have sufficed to produce a Sheridan. The broad, energetic humor of Vanbrugh’s excellent comedies secures him a significant position among the masters of the form.

But when he turned his hand to tragedy, the result was a strained, waxen mask, stiff and over-embellished, not unlike the preposterous airs of Lady Wishfort herself. And yet, those three golden comedies alone are enough—more than enough—to cement his place as a pillar of English letters, as timeless as the language in which he wrote. Without them, the art of comedy would lack its highest, most polished exemplars.

Ben Jonson, that restless reformer of the stage, tried to untangle the messy knot of comedy and tragedy that mirrored the chaos of life itself, but his comedies—though elaborate and ambitious—never danced with the grace that Congreve’s did. Among Congreve’s immediate forerunners, those who sought to graft a French elegance onto English soil, Wycherley’s work came off as too rough-hewn, too brutish, while Etherege’s was all feather and no weight, too insubstantial to stand beside his. Beyond them, the pickings were slim—little more than the raw, unvarnished energy of Shadwell, whose unruly vigor lacked the polish to ascend to greatness.

Time, the sole cause of my unrest,  
From whom I once hoped to be blessed,

Yet remain unmoved, unchanging,

The fault lies with you, not with me.

And joy in what you bestow.

I’ll exalt your dominion, unbroken, pure,  
And hymn your splendor, steadfast, sure,  
Your brilliance undimmed by loss.  

For as long as love persists, unspoiled, profound,  
Time will unveil treasures that astound—  
Riches immune to decay.

Time, that pitiless sculptor of all that shifts,  
Once a balm, a whispered gift,  
Now scores jagged wounds across my soul’s tender weave.  
You sparked the first fire, the ancient, wild glow,  
But now you stand—cold, aloof, as frozen stone—  
A silent monarch, distant, severe.  

Have I strayed from the path you etched in stars?  
Fallen prey to the mortal flaw that mars  
The fragile striving of our kind?  
Your unyielding cadence mocks my core,  
Shatters my calm, razes the shore  
Where once fullness stood, now only voids remain.  

Do not condemn this heart for daring to beat;  
It was your relentless pulse that wrote, complete,  
A love unblemished by grasping hands.  
The fleeting chase shadows that slip through light,  
But steadfast love roots deep, holds tight,  
Anchored in soil no storm can churn.  

Halt, sovereign Time, and yield but a breath,  
That joy might rise from the ruins of death—  
A haven where love’s voice still sings.  
I’ll praise your reign, unbroken, sublime,  
And weave you hymns of endless time,  
Your glory shining through all that’s lost.  

For while love endures, untouched, profound,  
Time’s hidden riches will still astound—  
Gifts no decay can claim.
